title: RA.1000 Bicep

translation.content: When Bicep, Printworks and Resident Advisor chose to collaborate on a livestream in 2018, no one could have predicted the response. The video, RA's most-viewed on YouTube with 4.5 million hits, has soundtracked countless pre-drinks, afters and poignant pandemic watch-alongs. Its magic, judging by the 110-plus comments, is a mix of impeccable flow, slamming drops and powerful swells of euphoria. As one joker put it, "I'm bald and I can feel my hair standing on end." This video captures Bicep on their ascent to joining the likes of Faithless, The Prodigy and The Chemical Brothers in the pantheon of great electronic live acts. For many, the set peaks with minutes to spare, as the Northern Irish duo tease in 2017's crossover smash "Glue." Many acts blessed with such an anthem would've been more than satisfied to rinse, repeat and rake in the profits. Not Andy Ferguson and Matt McBriar. If anything, achieving mainstream success—headlining Glastonbury's West Holts stage, hitting #2 in the UK Albums Chart, earning two BRIT Award nominations—has only hardened Bicep's resolve to keep innovating. The latest example is TAKKUUK, a new climate catastrophe-inspired collaboration with Indigenous vocalists from Greenland. This restless drive is coupled with a compellingly down-to-earth aura: the duo still make time to upload mixes from young artists to their prized, 17-year-old blog. A love of music and a desire to cultivate community have always trumped the clarion calls of fame. For two entirely normal Belfast lads, however, Bicep's music doesn't half reach for the stars. Their sound du jour remains a loud, melodic blend of ambient, breakbeat, trance and tech house, often layered with trippy vocals. That may sound overwhelming on paper, but for all the oomph and sugar highs there's a sensitivity and richness of sound design that can turn even the biggest arena shows into weirdly intimate experiences. And so we arrive at RA.1000, which, astonishingly, marks Bicep's debut on the RA Podcast. (The success of the RA Live may be partly responsible.) Their 55-minute mix is a perfect snapshot of the duo's dance floor might in action. And oh how they love a curveball. Anyone expecting lashings of twinkly synths will first have to journey through everything but: dub techno, screwface breaks, even some São Paulo garage. Then, a subtle tide of dazzling arpeggios, showing off Bicep's years-honed knack for moving clubs, venues and fields from gritty rave to stadium climax. – Carlos Hawthorn Can you share a mix that's particularly special to you? What makes it special? A mix that will forever be special is Optimo's House Party! on Clyde Built Radio. It dropped right at the beginning of the pandemic and was just on repeat getting us through those tough times. One evening a bunch of our friends all put it on at the same time and partied over Zoom to it. Very special, especially the second half. Following Matt's diagnosis and successful brian tumour operation, what has the last year looked like for you as a duo? We were straight into finishing our Takkuuk immersive audio-visual installation project with the charity In Place Of War, and working on all the Chroma releases all whilst back to touring at full pelt. It has been so great to be able to get so busy so quickly again. RA and Bicep have overlapped plenty over the years, but never a mix on the Podcast. Why is that, and what made the time right now? We did get the request a few times over the years but the timing was always just wrong for us, particularly whilst we were focusing on our live show so much over the past eight years. We weren't DJing week-in week-out, so felt the selections wouldn't be representative of what we would be hearing and seeing when we play. Now just felt like a time when we've been DJing and digging non-stop and we could do the series justice with a more representative selection. Like many Bicep projects which add muscle live, the Chroma era has been defined by hybrid A/V sets. Does an audio-only mix still hold quality for you? Absolutely; mixes have always been a chance to experience music in your own immersed world—going on a long bike ride, a walk, the gym and so on. While audiovisual works amazingly in clubs, music can tell its own story when listened to in isolation. In a world where technology is rapidly changing the way we perform, how do you see the future of DJing and mixing evolving? Tune selection and flow will always be more important than technology, which really just helps you express yourself. Increasingly more artists are filling their sets with their own productions and, for us, being able to make music just hours before a show has always been exciting. Beyond the instant value in getting your music to audiences, tune selection is much more important. Essentially, there needs to be strong ideas to begin with. Any word on when we might expect LP3? We'll be taking a nearly full year off touring from November here to work on the next album and live show! We really want to devote a lot of time to this one! Very excited.
translation.blurb: The adored arena duo go from gritty rave to stadium climax on RA.1000, their long-awaited RA Podcast debut.
